content.text: An agent system card is a structured document that describes an AI agent's capabilities, limitations, intended use, safety properties, and evaluation results in a format accessible to non-technical stakeholders. Where a model card describes a foundation model's training and performance characteristics, a system card describes a deployed agent system — the combination of model, tools, prompts, memory mechanisms, and operational constraints that together constitute what users and operators actually interact with. A customer support agent and a code review agent may use the same underlying language model but will have very different system cards because the tool access, guardrails, intended use, and risk profile differ entirely.

System cards serve multiple audiences: internal governance teams use them as compliance artifacts and risk assessment inputs; customer-facing versions inform enterprise buyers about the agents they are deploying; public versions demonstrate responsible AI practices to regulators and the broader public. The same underlying facts appear in each version, but the level of detail, the framing, and the sensitive information included differ by audience. Maintaining multiple audience-appropriate versions is itself a governance activity that must be built into the agent lifecycle.

attrs.alt: Agent system card structure showing nine sections: identity, capabilities, limitations, safety properties, evaluation results, intended use, data and privacy, maintenance history, and contact, with three audience versions (internal, customer, public)

content.text: Why System Cards Matter

As AI agents take autonomous actions — sending emails, executing database queries, filing documents, interacting with third-party APIs — the potential consequences of misuse, misconfiguration, or unexpected behavior scale significantly. System cards provide the documentation layer that makes these consequences legible: what can this agent actually do? What are the access permissions it holds? What happens when it encounters a scenario outside its training? What oversight mechanisms exist?

From a governance perspective, system cards are the primary accountability artifact for an agent. They are the document an auditor reviews when evaluating whether a deployed agent has been assessed for risk. They are the document a procurement team reviews when deciding whether to allow an agent to access production systems. They are the document a regulator examines when an agent causes harm and the organization must demonstrate it exercised due diligence before deployment.
Benefits
Creates a governance artifact that demonstrates pre-deployment risk assessment and informed deployment decisions.
Forces the agent team to articulate limitations honestly — the process of writing the limitations section surfaces gaps in testing and known failure modes.
Enables enterprise customers to make informed procurement and deployment decisions rather than relying on marketing materials.
Provides a reference document for incident investigation — when an agent behaves unexpectedly, the system card describes the intended behavior to compare against.
Supports regulatory compliance: EU AI Act conformity assessments require technical documentation that overlaps significantly with system card content.
Builds stakeholder trust by demonstrating transparent communication about capabilities and limitations.
Trade-offs
System cards require significant effort to create accurately and maintain over the agent lifecycle — the accuracy of a stale card can be worse than no card at all.
Disclosing limitations in a public card may provide information that bad actors use to probe the agent for exploits.
Multiple audience versions require processes to ensure that internal and external versions are consistent — contradiction between versions is itself a governance failure.
System cards can create liability if they overstate capabilities or omit known limitations that later cause harm — they are legal documents, not just engineering notes.
System Card Sections: What to Include
Section 1: System Identity

The identity section establishes the basic facts: the agent's name, current version, the team responsible, the underlying model(s) used, the deployment environment (production, staging, beta), and the date the card was last updated. Include the card version separate from the agent version — a card may be updated to add a new limitation finding without the agent itself changing.
Section 2: Capabilities

Describe what the agent can actually do: what tools it has access to (web search, database read, email send, code execution); the types of tasks it is designed to perform; the languages it operates in; the context window size and its implications for long-task performance; and the modalities it accepts (text, images, documents). Be specific — 'the agent can search the web' is less useful than 'the agent can execute web searches using the Brave Search API and retrieve up to 10 results per query.'
Section 3: Limitations

The limitations section is the most important and the most frequently omitted or understated. Honest limitations statements reduce downstream harm by preventing deployment in contexts where limitations will cause problems. Include: known accuracy bounds from evaluations; types of requests the agent handles poorly; temporal knowledge limitations (training data cutoff); languages or domains where performance degrades; failure modes observed in testing; and the hallucination rate for factual claims if measured.
Section 4: Safety Properties

Safety properties describe the mechanisms that prevent the agent from causing harm: input and output guardrails; refusal policies (what the agent will not do); escalation paths when the agent encounters a request it cannot or should not fulfill; human-in-the-loop requirements for specific action types; and override mechanisms for authorized users who need to adjust agent behavior within defined bounds.
Section 5: Evaluation Results

Summarize the evaluation methodology and key results from testing. Include: benchmark performance on relevant task types; red-team findings and how they were addressed; fairness and bias evaluation results across relevant demographic dimensions; and human evaluation scores where applicable. Reference the full evaluation report rather than re-stating all results in the system card.
Mental Model: The Nutrition Label

A system card is the nutrition label of an AI agent. Just as a food manufacturer must disclose what is in their product — including ingredients people might not want (allergens, additives) — an AI agent creator should disclose what the agent is made of, what it can do, what it cannot do, and what risks it carries. The nutrition label is required by regulation, but it also serves consumers who need to make informed decisions. A system card serves governance (required documentation) and users (informed deployment decisions) in the same way.
Audience Versions

The internal system card is the most detailed version. It includes the full limitations section with specific failure rates, any known security vulnerabilities and their mitigations, incident history and lessons learned, details of prompt engineering decisions that affect behavior, and access control information. This version is restricted to the AI team, security team, and compliance function.

The customer-facing version omits specific vulnerability details and internal incident history, but provides the capabilities, limitations, safety properties, data handling practices, and evaluation summary in enough detail for an enterprise buyer to make an informed deployment decision. Many enterprise AI procurement processes now require a system card (or equivalent documentation) as part of the vendor evaluation.

The public version provides a high-level overview suitable for general audiences: what the agent does, its key limitations, the safety measures in place, and how to report concerns. Major AI providers publish public system cards for their flagship agents; organizations deploying these agents in their own contexts should publish their own system cards describing the deployment context and any additional customizations.
How to Create and Maintain a System Card
Assign a system card owner during agent development — this is the person responsible for keeping the card accurate throughout the agent's life.
Create the initial system card as part of the pre-deployment readiness review, not as a post-hoc documentation task.
Populate the limitations section from the evaluation report, not from intuition — limitations must be grounded in actual observed behavior.
Review the safety properties section with the security team to confirm all guardrails described are actually implemented.

Define update triggers: the card must be updated when the agent is retrained, when new evaluation results are available, when tools are added or removed, and when a significant incident reveals an undisclosed limitation.
Version-control the system card in the same repository or documentation system as the agent code so version history is preserved.
Conduct a quarterly review to verify that the card remains accurate even if no update triggers have fired.
For enterprise customers, provide the customer-facing card in a discoverable location (vendor trust portal, documentation site) and notify customers when it is updated.
Code Example: System Card Schema and Generator

"""
agent_system_card.py
Defines a structured schema for agent system cards and generates 
audience-specific versions from a single source of truth.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional, Literal
import json

Audience = Literal["internal", "customer", "public"]

@dataclass
class EvaluationResult:
    benchmark_name: str
    score: float
    score_unit: str
    evaluation_date: date
    methodology_reference: str
    notes: str = ""

@dataclass
class ToolAccess:
    tool_name: str
    permissions: list[str]
    risk_level: Literal["low", "medium", "high", "critical"]
    authorization_required: bool
    description: str

@dataclass
class SafetyProperty:
    property_name: str
    description: str
    implementation: str
    bypass_possible: bool
    bypass_authorization: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class AgentSystemCard:
    # Identity
    agent_name: str
    version: str
    card_version: str
    owner_team: str
    base_model: str
    deployment_env: Literal["production", "staging", "beta", "internal"]
    release_date: date
    card_updated: date
    intended_use: str

    # Capabilities
    capabilities: list[str]
    tools: list[ToolAccess]
    input_modalities: list[str]
    output_modalities: list[str]
    context_window_tokens: int
    supported_languages: list[str]

    # Limitations (honest)
    limitations: list[str]
    known_failure_modes: list[str]
    training_data_cutoff: Optional[date]
    hallucination_rate_pct: Optional[float]
    out_of_scope_tasks: list[str]

    # Safety
    safety_properties: list[SafetyProperty]
    human_review_required_for: list[str]

    # Evaluation
    evaluation_results: list[EvaluationResult]

    # Data and privacy
    data_accessed: list[str]
    pii_handling: str
    prompt_log_retention_days: int
    training_data_description: str

    # Maintenance
    update_triggers: list[str]
    review_cadence: str

    # Contact
    contact_team: str
    contact_email: str
    issue_reporting_url: str

    # Internal-only fields
    known_vulnerabilities_and_mitigations: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    incident_history: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def to_dict(self, audience: Audience) -> dict:
        base = {
            "agent_name": self.agent_name,
            "version": self.version,
            "card_version": self.card_version,
            "card_updated": str(self.card_updated),
            "intended_use": self.intended_use,
            "capabilities": self.capabilities,
            "limitations": self.limitations,
            "safety_properties": [
                {
                    "name": s.property_name,
                    "description": s.description,
                }
                for s in self.safety_properties
            ],
        }
        if audience in ("internal", "customer"):
            base["tools"] = [
                {
                    "name": t.tool_name,
                    "permissions": t.permissions,
                    "risk_level": t.risk_level,
                }
                for t in self.tools
            ]
            base["evaluation_results"] = [
                {
                    "benchmark": e.benchmark_name,
                    "score": f"{e.score} {e.score_unit}",
                    "date": str(e.evaluation_date),
                }
                for e in self.evaluation_results
            ]
            base["data_handling"] = {
                "data_accessed": self.data_accessed,
                "pii_handling": self.pii_handling,
                "prompt_log_retention_days": self.prompt_log_retention_days,
            }
            base["known_failure_modes"] = self.known_failure_modes

        if audience == "internal":
            base["known_vulnerabilities"] = self.known_vulnerabilities_and_mitigations
            base["incident_history"] = self.incident_history
            base["out_of_scope_tasks"] = self.out_of_scope_tasks
            base["update_triggers"] = self.update_triggers

        return base

    def to_json(self, audience: Audience) -> str:
        return json.dumps(self.to_dict(audience), indent=2)

# Example system card
card = AgentSystemCard(
    agent_name="Customer Support Chatbot",
    version="2.4.1",
    card_version="3",
    owner_team="AI Platform Team",
    base_model="claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Anthropic API)",
    deployment_env="production",
    release_date=date(2025, 11, 1),
    card_updated=date(2026, 7, 19),
    intended_use="Handle tier-1 customer support inquiries for SaaS product, escalate unresolved issues to human agents",
    capabilities=[
        "Answer product documentation questions",
        "Create, update, and close support tickets via ITSM API",
        "Look up customer account status in CRM",
        "Escalate to human agent with context summary",
    ],
    tools=[
        ToolAccess("ITSM Ticket API", ["ticket:read", "ticket:write"], "medium", False, "Create and update support tickets"),
        ToolAccess("CRM Read API", ["customer:read"], "low", False, "Read customer account status"),
        ToolAccess("Human Escalation", ["escalate"], "low", False, "Transfer conversation to human agent"),
    ],
    input_modalities=["text"],
    output_modalities=["text"],
    context_window_tokens=200000,
    supported_languages=["English", "Spanish", "French"],
    limitations=[
        "Does not have access to financial transaction data",
        "Cannot process refund requests — escalates to billing team",
        "Knowledge of product features current as of training cutoff; may not reflect features released after that date",
        "Accuracy on technical troubleshooting degrades for edge-case hardware configurations",
    ],
    known_failure_modes=[
        "May suggest workarounds that no longer apply if product UI has changed since last knowledge refresh",
        "Occasionally generates ticket descriptions that miss context provided earlier in a long conversation",
    ],
    training_data_cutoff=date(2025, 8, 1),
    hallucination_rate_pct=1.8,
    out_of_scope_tasks=[
        "Legal advice", "Medical advice", "Financial advice", "Discussions outside product support scope",
    ],
    safety_properties=[
        SafetyProperty(
            "Output Guardrails",
            "All outputs are screened for harmful content before delivery",
            "Anthropic Constitutional AI + custom content policy layer",
            bypass_possible=False,
        ),
        SafetyProperty(
            "Refusal Policy",
            "Agent refuses out-of-scope requests and explains what it can help with",
            "System prompt constraint + fine-tuning",
            bypass_possible=False,
        ),
    ],
    human_review_required_for=["Refund requests", "Account deletion", "Security incident reports"],
    evaluation_results=[
        EvaluationResult("Task Completion Rate", 87.3, "%", date(2026, 6, 1), "Internal eval v2.4", ""),
        EvaluationResult("Hallucination Rate", 1.8, "%", date(2026, 6, 1), "Internal factual eval", ""),
        EvaluationResult("CSAT Score", 4.2, "/5", date(2026, 7, 1), "Production user survey", "n=1200"),
    ],
    data_accessed=["Customer account metadata", "Support ticket history", "Product documentation (read-only)"],
    pii_handling="Customer names and email addresses are accessed for account lookup; no PII is logged in prompt logs beyond session ID",
    prompt_log_retention_days=90,
    training_data_description="Fine-tuned on anonymized support ticket data from 2023-2025; base model training per Anthropic model card",
    update_triggers=["Model version change", "Tool access change", "New evaluation results", "Significant incident finding"],
    review_cadence="Quarterly review by AI Platform team; updated within 14 days of trigger events",
    contact_team="AI Platform Team",
    contact_email="ai-platform@company.com",
    issue_reporting_url="https://trust.company.com/report",
    known_vulnerabilities_and_mitigations=["Prompt injection via crafted ticket subject — mitigated by input sanitization in ticket intake"],
    incident_history=["2026-01-15: Session isolation bug exposed conversation fragments to other users; patched 2026-01-16"],
)

print("=== PUBLIC VERSION ===")
print(card.to_json("public"))
Real-World Example: Enterprise SaaS Deployment

A cloud data platform vendor deployed an AI SQL generation agent that translated natural language queries into SQL against customer databases. Before making it generally available, the vendor created system cards for three audiences. The internal card documented that the agent had been evaluated against 500 benchmark SQL queries with a 92% accuracy rate, but had a known failure mode with subqueries involving more than three join levels; this limitation was tracked as a P2 engineering priority. The customer card described the 92% accuracy rate, the subquery limitation, the read-only database access (the agent could read but not write), and the data handling practices. The public card described the agent as an AI-assisted SQL generation tool with human review recommended for production queries. Enterprise customers consistently cited the customer-facing system card as a key factor in their procurement decision, noting it was the first AI vendor document they had seen that honestly described limitations alongside capabilities.
Common Mistakes
Omitting the limitations section entirely or writing it as a brief disclaimer — the limitations section is the most governance-critical section of the card.
Creating the card after deployment rather than as a pre-deployment gate — post-hoc documentation often omits the honest assessment that only happens under pre-deployment scrutiny.
Treating the system card as a marketing document written by product or marketing teams without technical review — marketing language about 'powerful AI' is not a limitation statement.
Failing to update the card when the agent is retrained or its tools change — a card that describes an outdated version is worse than no card.
Not distinguishing between the underlying model's properties (described in the model card) and the deployed agent's properties (described in the system card).
Best Practices
Make the system card a required artifact for the pre-deployment review — no deployment approval without a completed and reviewed system card.
Write the limitations section based on actual evaluation results, not intuition — run the evaluations first, then write the card.
Use the same version control system for system cards as for agent code so that card versions are traceable to agent versions.
Establish a quarterly review calendar entry for each system card so drift from the current deployment state is caught regularly, not only when update triggers fire.
For customer-facing cards, have legal review the document for accuracy and liability implications before publishing.
Publish the customer-facing system card in your vendor trust portal with a public URL that customers can cite in their own compliance documentation.
Performance and Scalability

As agent portfolios grow, system card maintenance becomes a significant ongoing activity. Organizations with five or more agents should implement a system card registry — a searchable inventory of all deployed agents with links to their system cards, current versions, and next review dates. GRC platforms are beginning to support agent system card templates as a compliance module. Automated tooling that extracts key metrics (evaluation scores, hallucination rates) from monitoring systems and propagates them to the system card reduces the manual update burden.
Comparison with Alternatives
Document Type

Governance Value
Agent System Card (this)
Deployed agent system

Researchers, auditors

Engineers

Specific to data compliance
Security and Reliability

System cards that describe tool access permissions, guardrail bypass conditions, and known failure modes are sensitive documents. The internal version must be access-controlled — it should not be published publicly or shared with customers. The customer version should describe safety properties at a level of detail that enables informed deployment without providing a specific attack surface map. The public version should avoid any specificity that could assist adversarial exploitation.
Key Takeaways
An agent system card describes the deployed agent system — capabilities, limitations, safety properties, and evaluation results — not just the underlying model.
The limitations section is the most governance-critical section: it must reflect actual evaluation findings, not optimistic marketing language.
Three audience versions are required: internal (full detail), customer (capabilities, limits, data handling), and public (high-level overview and safety properties).
System cards must be created as pre-deployment artifacts, not post-hoc documentation — the process of writing the card surfaces gaps in evaluation and safety testing.
Update triggers must be defined and enforced: model version changes, tool access changes, new evaluation results, and significant incidents all require card updates.
Version-control system cards alongside agent code so that the card state is traceable to the agent state at any point in its history.
EU AI Act conformity assessments require technical documentation that overlaps significantly with system card content — a well-maintained system card simplifies regulatory compliance.
